text_clean: Centro turístico por excelencia en Perú. Es un lugar con bastante historia. La Plaza Mayor de Lima está bastante iluminado y tiene banquetas donde puedes descansar con total tranquilidad. Alrededor de la Plaza está el Palacio municipal de Lima, la Catedral de Lima y el Palacio de Gobierno. Hay bastante información sobre el lugar en Internet. Mencionar que no ingresan vehículos (autos ni motos) por las calles circundantes, así que es necesario aparcar en lugares más alejados. Hay estacionamientos pagados cercanos. Puedes pasar con bicicletas y a pie. La Plaza está bien conservada y limpia, aunque nunca faltan las personas que ensucian indiscriminadamente. La Plaza es segura, cuenta con vigilancia municipal y policial. Las imágenes son de noviembre 2022.

text_clean: The visit to Machu Picchu was amazing and breath taking. advisable to wear comfy clothing and sneakers. text_clean: There are no words to accurately describe the majesty of this city in the clouds. As you enter into the city, you feel transported to a new world especially in the morning before the fog has faded. We spent two days at the city. On the first, we hiked Waynapicchu. It is a difficult climb especially at fairly high elevation but you can take your time. If you are able, you must do this hike. It was the highlight of our Peru trip. Sadly, some of the sites were closed for renovation including the Moon Temple. On the second, we explored the city with a tour guide. We booked ours through Adios Adventure Travel and they were great though a little pricy. Even when exploring the city, prepare for a lot of walking and stairs. Llamas (and a few alpacas) wander aroud freely. Make sure you use the restroom prior to entering the city. There is no in and out privilege. You probably should take the bus from Aguas Calientes to Macchu Picchu especially if you expect the walk around Macchu Picchu to be strenous and tiring already. We walked down the mountain hoping to see an Andea bear. It was still beautiful but not a crucial hike. Make sure you purchase your tickets for Macchu Picchu in advance. There are a very limited amount of tickets sold at the gate hence the protests. We were able to avoid waiting in long lines and have security knowing our spot was booked by buying tickets several weeks prior.

text_clean: Peru Rail and Inca Rail are the two train operators. I went with Inca Rail’s bimodal service based on trip times. I took The Voyager light option. Overall, it was a good experience. I took a bus from Cusco to Ollantaytambo, then a train to Aguas Calientes. I went the day before to avoid any potential delays. You can book everything as a package through either company, but I found it cheaper and more flexible to book tickets separately. You can either walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu or take the bus ($12 USD one way). The buses are run by Consettur, and tickets can be bought online or at the ticket office. The ride takes about 25 minutes, and I recommend getting in line at least an hour before your entrance time. There are signs for different lines based on entry times. Make sure you have your ticket and passport before getting in line. While waiting, guides will offer their services. I was quoted $25 USD per person for a group of four or $100 USD for a private tour. I got in line around 7 am and reached the entrance around 8. Once you arrive, there’s a bathroom that costs 2 soles. There are no official bathrooms inside. There’s also a spot to get food and store luggage or a bag if needed. I visited at the end of January and was worried about the weather, so I booked two different routes on two separate days. I did the 2A classic route and 1A Machu Picchu Mountain. I had an 8 am entry time both days and thankfully had great weather. It seemed to get cloudier and rainier in the afternoon The 2A route was good. I went without a guide, but I heard others say they really enjoyed having one. It seemed like about half the visitors had guides, half didn’t. It took me about two hours to walk around, taking my time. The 2A classic route is popular for a reason, but I didn’t see any signs differentiating it from 2B, just a general sign for route 2. The highlight for me was hiking Machu Picchu Mountain. It wasn’t crowded at all. I saw maybe 10 people during the hike. The views were stunning, and while the hike is challenging, it’s absolutely worth it. You’re walking up stone steps for pretty much the entire way, so wearing shoes or hiking boots with good traction is a must. The trail is well-maintained. There are great viewpoints along the hike, so even if you can’t make it all the way up, you’ll still get good views. The first 75% is wide, then it gets narrower and steeper with some drop-offs, but nothing too intense. Make sure to bring water, bug spray, a raincoat, poncho, and sunscreen. Once you reach the top, you get a bird’s-eye view of the whole area, and it’s incredible. It took me about 1.5 hours to hike up and 1 hour to hike down.

Este circuito de aguas, ganó un premio a nivel internacional, por lo que era! Nosotros mismos no cuidamos lo bueno que tenemos, nos falta educación, cultura, respeto.

text_clean: El lugar está lindo, con atracciones y fuentes para niños y adultos. Lo malo: afuera había fila y una señora que trabajaba ahí salía con un megáfono a regañar a las personas de la fila. Fue muy desagradable escuchar sus gritos, en especial cuando uno se apresta a divertirse. Luego, adentro hay mucho comercio ambulante y te cobran por entrar a otros recintos, incluyendo el baño (es insólito, porque ya te cobran por entrar al parque). Hay un espectáculo de luz, agua y sonido, pero empezó muy tarde, 45 minutos después, y eso hizo que la gente se aburriera y, en general, causará decepción. Ojo: adentro hay un mariposario y dejan las luces encendidas hasta tarde para que las mariposas no se duerman y la gente pueda verlas (previo pago, por supuesto). Eso es generarle estrés a las mariposas por unos soles. No volvería, aunque es entretenido.

text_clean: El pueblo de aguas Calientes me pareció hermoso, me parece que es un lugar bastante hermoso pero exageradamente costoso y con un poco de falta de cultura, al caminar pude ver cómo un lugar de estos que es patrimonio de la humanidad se encuentra lleno de basura y contaminación tanto por los locales como por los mismos turistas que van a visitar a este bello lugar, también es un lugar en el que todo te lo quieren cobrar en dólares y conseguir boletería es bastante complejo y difícil de conseguir, en ocasiones no se consigue ni estando en el mismo lugar, mi forma de ver a Machu Picchu fue el subir una de las montañas que está al frente, una ruta hermosa pero algo complicada debido a que cortaron los anclajes y escaleras, solo quedan dos cuerdas y dos Guayas que sirven de ayuda el resto toca escalar bajo su propio riesgo. La forma fácil de ir por su propia cuenta es comprar un tiquete en la terminal terrestre que va a Quillabamba y tienes que bajarte en santa María y que tiene un valor de 25 soles por persona, el bus se toma en la terminal de Quillabamba y un taxi te cobra 6 soles, en santa María se toma un colectivo hasta santa Teresa que te cobrará 10 soles por persona y de santa Teresa a hidro electrica te cobrarán 5 soles por persona y de ahí solo queda caminar hasta llegar al pueblo de aguas calientes.

Lo único que deberían hacer es aprovechar las taquillas vacías para colocar cajeras en los días que son más concurridos por personas residenciales y turistas. Es la única cosa negativa. text_clean: ¡¿Que más se puede decir de este lugar maravilloso?! :D Ver la ciudad inca de Machupicchu es sin duda un imperdible en tu viaje por Perú. Es importante que compres tu ticket de entrada con meses de anticipación, sobre todo si quieres subir al Huaynapicchu. Si solo quieres visitar la ciudadela, entonces puedes comprar la entrada con unos cuantos días de antes. Te recomendamos entrar en el horario de las 6am ya que a esa hora la mayoría de los visitantes están haciendo fotos y la ciudadela está vacía. Ademas, con un poco de suerte podrás ver pequeñas nubes de neblina que harán mejor tus fotos. Para subir puedes hacerlo a pie o en bus. Es recomendable pagar el bus de ida y bajar las escalinatas de regreso. Así evitarás llegar cansado a Machupicchu. No olvides llevar suficiente agua, pequeños snack, gorra y protector solar. (Todo en una pequeña mochila para que puedas guardar tu basura). En teoría la entrada con guía es obligatoria, sin embargo puedes optar entrar por tu cuenta, hacer todas las fotos que quieras y cuando estés listo unirte a algún grupo pidiendo permiso al guía y por supuesto dando tu respectivo pago. Recuerda que los baños solo están a la entrada al igual que el servicio de guarda equipajes. Al salir no te olvides de poner tu sello en el pasaporte como recuerdo de tu visita a Machupicchu ;)

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.

text_clean: Unbelievable in person. Pictures do not do it justice. Here’s some helpful tips as an American tourist on their first trip to Macchu Picchu: 1 Buy your tickets well in advance. There is a huge line at the tourist office in Aguas Calientes for last minute ticket purchases. It’s chaotic, skip the headache and plan ahead. We were out in Aguas Calientes for two nights and both nights the line / crowd was huge. Do your research on the best time to go. We went early October (last week) - the temperature was perfect. Crispy in the early morning then warmed up later on. Sun is strong, wear sunscreen and bug spray. 2 If you can, and are up for the challenge, do the hiking trail up. Not the Inca Trail. There’s a walking path that’s about ~1+ hour (longer if you take breaks). It’s 100% worth the effort for the views. It’s like the stair-master on hard. There are buses that run very frequently up but they’re not for the faint of heart or for impatient people who hate waiting in long lines. The buses fly up the sides of the mountain (no thank you). 3 Get an early ticket. It gets crowded. We had a 9am and it was great. It was crowded when we were leaving about 2 hours later. 4 There’s a snack counter and a formal restaurant. The snack counter is 10/10 with beer, cocktails, desserts, and snacks (chicken empanada is A1). Grab something before you go in as you can’t eat when you’re in there. 5 You cannot get in ahead of your ticket time. They’re strict about this. 6 BRING YOUR PASSPORT 7 Get a guide. There’s no informational signs that describe what you’re looking at. If you don’t have someone describing things you’re just there for the views (even though we left with more questions than answers) 8 Bring soles for the bathroom. 2 soles to enter. 9 Respect the beauty of the site

text_clean: My Peru experience was amazing and undertaking the Inca Trail to Machu Picchu one of the most wonderful experiences of my life. Camping and waking up to such beautiful mountain views in the Andes, will stay with me forever. It's a tough walk but one I'd recommend to anyone wanting to see and feel some of our beautiful planet. To finally arrive at the Sun Gate and see Machu Picchu for the first time, at dawn, just, wow. The amusing part is then walking around this breathtaking place, after 5 days of basically living rough, filthy and covered in bug bites amongst all the clean tourists who have got the train up, makes you feel quite self conscious lol!! So I'd say walking the Inca Trail is the ultimate way to experience this, if you can. If not, a day trip via train is certainly to be recommended just to get to see Machu Picchu itself.

text_clean: Pristine from start to finish. The train is a clean and comfortable 3.5-4 hour ride from Cusco. Following the train ride, you will enter Machu Pichu City where you will take a short bus ride to the historical site. Please beware, you will still have a fairly strenuous walk/hike from the bus to the site. If you live in a flat area, I suggest using a stair climber to prepare yourself. My tour, which I booked through AirBnB experiences was a one on one, personalized experience. This allowed me to rest when needed, ask questions, and make correlations. Most of the groups were 20+ people, I don't suggest that. You want to observe and absorb, so book an intimate tour. Also, important to note, there are 3 tour levels. Level one only grants access to having your picture taken close to the site, without touring the site. Level 2 grants the most access overall, but it does not focus on the royalty sites like Level 3. There is also an option to add a hike to Winchu Pichu (the mountain in the background of the pictures). That option is only available twice per day. I would never, so I'm not sure how to book it 😆🤣😆🤣 PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE bring only minimal gear, otherwise you will be rucking. A sweat suit with poncho or a lightweight all weather jacket will suffice. Layer the clothing so you can add/subtract as needed. Apply bug spray before leaving your hotel. Carry only one bottle of water. Fanny pack + water is all you need. You can buy snacks before/after in the city. YOU MUST HAVE YOUR PASSPORT TO ENTER/ EXIT THE SITE. P.S. entry is limited to a few thousand people per day, please book well in advance to secure your spot. Also, the rainy season starts in October. I was very blessed to only endure a sprinkle. With a hard rain, you won't be able to see and it will be very dangerous. You are walking/hiking on stones the entire time.

text_clean: Pretty dope! For all those unfamiliar there are only 2 ways to get to the small town at the base of MP, by train ($150+ USD round trip from cusco) or by foot (very expensive multi day trip). It costs about $150 round trip to fly to Cusco. Entrance to MP is around $40+ USD. From Aguas calientes (small town as the base of MP) you can hike up or take a bus ($24 USD each way). Recommend getting the wynapicchu ticket ($65-70 USD with entry), it lets you reenter after your hike. Pretty good option if you do the 10am hike, you can go through with your guide, hike, exit to eat and hydrate and then reenter to take your time. Private guides cost about $70 USD for a private 2-3hour tour. Make sure they have their ID badge. Guides have to be certified by the government, so check them so you don’t get skunked! Group tours are up to 14 people and range $10-20 USD per person. Guides take local and foreign currency. Make sure to speak with them a bit in depth to make sure they speak your language. I’ve heard of bilingual tours where one language group recurved much more information than the other. There is no food allowed inside MP, and there are no bathrooms in MP, you have to exit to use to toilet and standard tickets don’t allow reentry. You also cannot backtrack, there are staff that watch to make sure people move through. There are certain sections such as the temple of the condor that are only open for a limited amount of time. Recommend hitting the sun temple in the am, hike and then seeing the condor temple on the way out as this closes at 1pm. You can also hike the Inca ridge once inside for free (40 minute round trip) or the sun gate for free (2 hour round trip). Some people like to get in early to watch the sunrise through the sun gate. There are llamas all over the site, they are really friendly. Bring LOTS of water, especially if you’re hiking. They sell small water bottles for about $2.50. Sometimes you can use card for water and snacks. It depends whose working at the ice cream stand. They woman working clearly didn’t like tourists and told us cash only. We walked past 10 minutes later and she was using a card for a local... lol. Pretty magical place! Make sure to do some strength training and cardio beforehand. Eat a huge breakfast before you get on the bus. Oh and just be prepared to wait in line for your bus with literally 1000 other people going up at the same time. I find that knowing tips ahead of time helps with the anxiety of a strange place.

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

text_clean: Fuimos a la salida de un curso el día domingo, y esta fue mi segunda vez asistiendo. Sin embargo, por segunda vez consecutiva noté los mismos detalles que deberían mejorar. Los recorridos son demasiado planos y se vuelven algo monótonos, además de que los grupos son muy grandes, lo que hace que no todos los visitantes presten atención o se sientan incluidos. En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.

text_clean: Increíble lugar, aunque sea una vez en la vida debemos visitar! Para visitar MP, sacar las entradas con varios días de anticipación, ahora se hace todo desde la web de Machu Picchu, se puede pagar con tarjeta o en las oficinas que están cerca de la plaza Regocijo. Atención con el horario, porque solo se puede entrar en el horario especifico. Aun así, si te toca el último turno y no te conviene, puedes entrar hasta las 7:25 am siempre y cuando vayas con un guía. El guía cobra 20 soles por persona o 100 por un grupo familiar. Ya estando allí, habrá varios guardianes que verán que no se saquen el tapabocas en ningún momento. Lo niños tienen más tolerancia en ese aspecto. Está todo muy bien cuidado y señalizado. El detalle que malogra todo el paseo es el tren local, que como turistas nacionales tenemos la opción de tomar . Sin embargo, la tensión por conseguir un boleto es terrible, no debería ser así ya que al final todos viajan, la caseta de serenazgo te da un turno y con eso deberías evitar colas. Hay mucha especulación y también eso da pie para que algunos taxistas te quieran llevar a otras estaciones tratando de sacar ventaja e incluso engañar a los turistas. Tengan cuidado con eso. Fuera de eso, todo excelente 👍

Tambien hacen paseos en caballo con carruaje, es muy elegante y bonito aunque hay muchas críticas por el maltrato animal evidenciado en redes sociales, en esta ocasión pude visualizar tranquilidad en los animalitos y sobretodo su espacio para que tomen agua, el costo del paseo es de 7 soles por persona. Igual, antes de contratar el servicio verifiquen que sean buenos y respetuosos con los caballitos, hay que consumir lo bueno, sin dañar a terceros.
sugerencia_heuristica: negativo (heurística por estrellas 1-2; REQUIERE verificación humana)

text_clean: Todo peruano debe conocer este lugar!!! Es excelente y sobre todo barato... Si vienes desde Chachapoyas puedes tomar tours q cobran entre 90 a 110 soles x persona todo incluido, hasta almuerzos... Por cuenta propia tomas una combi a las 6am a 15 soles aprox hasta el distrito de Nuevo Tingo, luego caminas 15 minutos hasta el local de embarque de transporte para el ingreso hacia los TELEFÉRICOS donde compras tú entrada (20.40 soles precio, incluye ida y vuelta) IMPORTANTE.. NO PERDER LOS TICKETS xq si los pierdes, compras otros para el regreso al precio inicial... una vez pagado, esperas tú turno y ellos te llevan en bus hasta la zona elevada del cerro para subir por fin a los telefericos, el trayecto es de 20 minutos aprox, luego te hacerlas a la casa amarilla donde pagas tú entrada a 30 soles y te permiten subir el camino de 2 km q demoran unos 45 minutos... Ya en la cima vaz a la derecha hasta el ingreso a la fortaleza donde entregas la entrada a compraste a 30 soles... Una vez adentro, DISFRUTA DEL PASEO, debes calcular tus tiempos xq el ticket del teleférico te menciona la hora de regreso... Así q atento... Igual te dan tolerancia pero si hay mucha gente puede q se compliquen los tiempos pues la cola se hace grande al regreso. Pásenla chvrs causitas! Sí desean alguien que les guíe en el tour les dejo el número de contacto del guía que sale en la foto de chaleco beige

text_clean: Machu Picchu: A Must-See Wonder, with a Few Tips Machu Picchu truly lives up to the hype – it's a place of breathtaking beauty and incredible history. Hiring a guide at the bus station was the best decision we made. Our guide was not only incredibly knowledgeable, weaving tales of the Incas into the very stones, but she was also wonderful with our children and a whiz at getting those perfect family photos. A word of advice for those not accustomed to high altitudes: spend a day or two acclimating in Cusco beforehand. The city offers a wealth of activities and attractions, so you won't be bored. Trust me, it makes a huge difference at Machu Picchu. Speaking of getting to Machu Picchu, the bus situation from Aguas Calientes can be a bit chaotic. Signage isn't great, and boarding can feel rushed. We traveled with Inca Rail, and while the train itself was comfortable, the waiting area was overcrowded and a bit messy. Overall: Machu Picchu is an unforgettable experience. Just be prepared for the altitude and a bit of a scramble getting there. Tips: Hire a guide: It's worth it for the knowledge, stories, and photo assistance. Acclimate: Spend time in Cusco if you're not used to high altitudes. Manage expectations: The bus situation and train station can be less than ideal. Make sure tu buy your ticket in advance.

text_clean: 6-7 hour bus ride from Arequipa (terminal terrestre, Arturo Ibanez 130) to Cabanaconde. Route over highlands, vicuna reserve, down to Chivay in canyon, then down canyon, (best views from right side of bus). 9:30 AM Arequipa departure (Andelusia) and nights in Cabanaconde before and after self guided trek, seemed better than 3:00 AM Arequipa departure, short last trek day and back to Arequipa of tours. Many places to stay, I was at Pachamama hostel about 2 blocks up from the plaza on San Pedro St. Good information about trek options and good place to meet others doing unguided treks. I did 2d/1n trek, 1st day to Llahuar with scenic descent along canyon edge, then down into side canyon, final descent to Colca river crossing, climb into Taruna, back down to Llahuar on the Colca river just after crossing the Huaruro river. Nice bungalows on river, friendly staff, fixed menu dinner, breakfast and sandwichs available, all at very reasonable prices. Natural hot pools and swimming pool right on river not open because river very high in early March. Very ambitious new "restaurant" construction ongoing. As hike to Llahuar mostly down and not too long, I was there well before noon. Could just chill in nice location. I opted for day hike up Huaruro canyon that starts steeply right from Llahuar then more gradually up to Llatica where a bridge crosses the river. From there another steep climb to high trail that contours back down other side of canyon to above Belen. Nice safe and scenic canyon trails. Down main street in Belen past soccer field to steep trail to Paclla. Cross road and continue on trail down through terraced gardens that eventually goes to Taruna above Llahuar. This added another 11 km and 700m vertical, about 4 hours at a fast pace, to first day. 2nd day back up to Belen, options are trail short cuts to Paclla not too steep, then very steep to Belen, or longer but more gradually walk on road. From Belen you are on road up to viewpoint bend then level to shortcut trail down to plateau, join main trail down to Sangalle (relatively touristy and expensive, but amazing given that everything has to come in by horse or burro). That just leaves the 2.5 - 3.5 hour all uphill switchbacks to plateau and final km to Cabanaconde. Total for 2nd day a healthy 16-18km with 1700m elevation gain. If I was doing it again, would spend 2 nights in canyon, 1st at Llahuar, then 2nd up at Fure with day hike up to the waterfall (cataratas), day 3 high trail back from Fure contouring back to join road at high point above Sangalle, then through Sangalle up and out. Another interesting high altitude strenuous option might be continuing up from Fure to Tocallo at 3800m where some maps show a trail that goes in and out of several canyons with final pass then long descent to Malata above Sangalle. Another night in canyon and some local information about condition of trails would be needed. Canyon country was unique, scenic and very green with lots of water in the rainy season. Lodging and meals in many areas down in canyon allows for very light packs, a plus given all the climbs. No reservations needed for anything during rainy season, probably a different story in high season. A minibus service that goes on the not too scary dirt road from Carbanaconde, crossing the Rio Colca at the same place my trek did has beautiful brand new rain sheltered stops at all the little towns all the way to Tapay! This allows some less strenuous options for the canyon that are sure to become more popular.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Anímate a visitar las enigmáticas Líneas de Nasca Las Líneas de Nasca son unos curiosos geoglifos (figuras construidas en cerros usando piedras). Se encuentran localizadas en las Pampas de Jumana, en el desierto de Nasca, entre esta ciudad y Palpa en Perú. Algunas de las líneas poseen un ancho de entre los 40 y los 210 centímetros y se extienden por 520 km², y otras incluso se prolongan hasta un área de 800 km². El principal atractivo son las Líneas de Nasca, las cuales se pueden ver desde una avioneta o subiendo a las colinas cercanas, dado que estas se han mantenido en buen estado debido al buen clima que tiene la ciudad. Asimismo, puedes visitar museos y los alrededores del lugar. El costo aproximado del pasaje en avioneta bordea los USD 100 dólares por persona, a lo que se le suma unos 30 soles por impuestos aeroportuarios. Conviene hacer el recorrido aéreo durante las primeras horas de la mañana, ya que es cuando suele estar más despejado y hay menos viento, lo que favorecerá tu visibilidad y te permitirá tomar mejores fotos. En promedio, el recorrido dura unos 30 minutos, y en el trayecto podrás observar 12 figuras, entre ellas el mono, la araña, el pelícano, entre otros. No te preocupes mucho por el clima, no por nada Nazca es conocida como «la ciudad del eterno verano». El clima es soleado y seco durante todo el año, con una temperatura promedio de 23°C aunque puede llegar hasta los 30°C entre los meses de enero y marzo. Si vas a sobrevolar las Líneas de Nazca es importante que lleves una casaca cortavientos, pues puede haber vientos huracanados en el desierto. Recuerda que el vuelo dura unos 30 minutos aproximadamente (lleva pastillas para el mareo, por si acaso). Al ser un clima soleado y cálido, no olvides tu bloqueador, lentes de sol y otros objetos “veraniegos”. Si eres de los que temen las turbulencias, te recomendamos volar las Líneas de Nazca por la mañana, y así evitar los fuertes vientos de la tarde. Que te diviertas..!!! Si te gusto la información dale "Like"

En promedio, el recorrido dura unos 30 minutos, y en el trayecto podrás observar 12 figuras, entre ellas el mono, la araña, el pelícano, entre otros. No te preocupes mucho por el clima, no por nada Nazca es conocida como «la ciudad del eterno verano». El clima es soleado y seco durante todo el año, con una temperatura promedio de 23°C aunque puede llegar hasta los 30°C entre los meses de enero y marzo. Si vas a sobrevolar las Líneas de Nazca es importante que lleves una casaca cortavientos, pues puede haber vientos huracanados en el desierto. Recuerda que el vuelo dura unos 30 minutos aproximadamente (lleva pastillas para el mareo, por si acaso). Al ser un clima soleado y cálido, no olvides tu bloqueador, lentes de sol y otros objetos “veraniegos”. Si eres de los que temen las turbulencias, te recomendamos volar las Líneas de Nazca por la mañana, y así evitar los fuertes vientos de la tarde. Que te diviertas..!!! Si te gusto la información dale "Like"

text_clean: Machu Picchu is an ancient Incan citadel located high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. The site is one of the most iconic and breathtakingly beautiful destinations in the world, attracting millions of visitors every year. To reach Machu Picchu, visitors can either hike the famous Inca Trail or take a train from the nearby town of Cusco. Once there, visitors can explore the ruins of the citadel, which include impressive structures such as temples, terraces, and plazas. The intricate stonework and engineering feats of the Incas are truly awe-inspiring. While Machu Picchu can get crowded, especially during peak season, there are ways to avoid the crowds and make the most of the experience. One option is to visit early in the morning or later in the afternoon when there are fewer visitors. Another option is to take a guided tour, which can provide insight into the history and significance of the site. Overall, Machu Picchu is a must-see destination for anyone visiting Peru or South America. The beauty, history, and cultural significance of the site are truly unmatched, and it's an experience that will stay with you for a lifetime. Para llegar a Machu Picchu, los visitantes pueden hacer la famosa Camino Inca o tomar un tren desde la cercana ciudad de Cusco. Una vez allí, los visitantes pueden explorar las ruinas de la ciudadela, que incluyen impresionantes estructuras como templos, terrazas y plazas. La intrincada obra de piedra y los logros de ingeniería de los incas son realmente impresionantes. Aunque Machu Picchu puede llenarse de turistas, especialmente durante la temporada alta, hay formas de evitar las multitudes y aprovechar al máximo la experiencia. Una opción es visitar temprano en la mañana o más tarde por la tarde, cuando hay menos visitantes. Otra opción es tomar un recorrido guiado, que puede proporcionar información sobre la historia y la importancia del sitio. En general, Machu Picchu es un destino imperdible para cualquier persona que visite Perú o América del Sur. La belleza, la historia y la importancia cultural del sitio son realmente incomparables, y es una experiencia que permanecerá contigo para toda la vida.

text_clean: A very beautiful trekking area and unlike anywhere else in Peru. We spent two days trekking this area without a guide or tour, which is totally unnecessary in this canyon. The trails couldn’t be easier to follow and the whole area is perfect for fit hikers but beginner trekkers. Don’t be fooled though, the steep drop into the canyon and painful return climb to the rim are harder than most hikes, and reminiscent of those found in the Grand Canyon. We did the long classic route which descends to Llahuar and loops to San Juan before climbing back up to Cabanaconde. This is typically done over three days but we got to Llahuar by 10:00 on day one so competed the second days hike as well. That day was around 20+ kilometers and tough but rewarding. The next morning, we climbed from San Juan to the rim, which was quite a bit longer and tougher than expected. We had decided to skip San Galle which is on most trek itineraries as it is the most touristy of the villages in the canyon. We stayed at Colibri Lodge for our night inside, which was one of the nicer guesthouse we found in South America. No reservation, just got lucky with a walk in and they had availability for us. The only downside of trekking here are the ridiculously limited bus options. Unless you are coming from or going to Arequipa, there really aren’t any public buses available. There are a handful of tourist buses, but they all make lame stops at expensive restaurants, viewpoints, etc and cost $50-65 USD for Puno or Cusco. We didn’t want to go all the way back to Arequipa since we were headed to Cusco, and managed to find a public bus option to get there without backtracking. It included a few steps and a little adventure, but cost just 50 soles and was actually pretty easy. And let’s face it, you wouldn’t be trekking in Peru if you didn’t like adventure. If you want specific info on the trekking routes and distances, how to do this without a guide, or most importantly all of the transport options for accessing the canyon and then getting to places other than Arequipa, search our name, Career Break Adventures and Colca Canyon or something of that nature. Have fun and enjoy the trek!

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: n un desierto de Nazca, se encuentran estos enigmáticos dibujos y líneas que los arqueólogos las denominan Geogliflos y que hasta el día de hoy no encuentran una explicación del como y el porque fueron construidos, solo como un detalle no son visibles desde el nivel del suelo, fueron hechos para ser vistos desde lo alto y las evidencias de las culturas de esa época, evidenciaban de que hubieran necesitado tecnologías superiores que no les correspondería ni a la época ni lugar. Según información de nuestro guía, las líneas rectas son tan perfectas que su desviación geométrica medida, no llegan al 2 por 1000 (2 metros por cada kilómetro) La Líneas de Nazca más representativas son los figuras de animales, entre ellos: colibríes, cóndores, garza, la grulla, el pelícano, gaviota, mono, araña, caracol, perro con patas y cola larga, figura antropomorfa, dos llamas, etc. Reptiles como el lagarto, cortado por la carretera Panamericana Sur, la iguana, lagartija, y una serpiente. Además de Líneas rectas a manera de pistas de aterrizaje, mezclado con espirales. Su perfecto estado de conservación se debe María Reiche Neumann, alemana que dedico su vida a limpiar y literalmente barrer las líneas para evitar su destrucción y es gracias a ella que la Unesco inscribió a “ Las líneas y Geogliflos de Nazca y de Pampas de Jumana” como Patrimonio de la Humanidad.

text_clean: Machu Picchu ⛰ La forma más barata de visitar Machu Picchu es teniendo tiempo: ⛰ De Cusco tomar un transfer hasta Ollantaytambo temprano en la mañana y el trayecto dura 1 hora con 30 minutos. La agencia te recoge en el hospedaje. ⛰ Luego otro viaje en tren local y como es tan solicitado por los propios Peruanos, toca hacer la reserva con tiempo para poder encontrar cupos; la prioridad es para los locales. ⛰ Para subir a las ruinas, el paquete incluye la entrada pero si llegas por ti mismo; existen dos formas: la paga y la gratis. Puedes usar el bus que vale para extranjeros 12 dólares por trayecto. Aceptan efectivo y tarjeta. El viaje tarda 25 minutos. ⛰ La forma gratis es subir toda la montaña en un sendero señalizado y la subida dura casi tres horas. La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.

La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

text_clean: This is a magical place to visit. If you have made the effort to travel all this way on multiple modes of transport I’m sure you will be as excited as me when you catch your first glimpse of Machu Picchu. It was suggested that although you can walk up from the town you may be better taking the 35 min bus ride. I’m glad we did. The buses run all day every 10-15min so the queue does move quickly but def start to queue an hour before your entry time. DONT FORGET YOUR PASSPORT! It’s not a difficult hike / walk if you doing the main area but pack water, rain jacket and sun protection. Only small bags allowed. No walking sticks (but they really are not needed). The tickets are +- $25 per entry. We got a guide at the entrance 100sol per person. She was amazingly knowledgeable and we were with her for 3hrs. Money well spent - we came away appreciating the experience even more. We had been giving the suggestion of route 2 that goes a bit higher up than route 1 but still covers most of route 1. Guide confirmed it was a good choice. We going back tomorrow to do Wayna Picchu mountain ($50 per entry for this hike) and will do route 4 before climbing the mountain (which you do need to be fit and prepared for). FYI - The temple of the moon is still closed since Covid as of today. I’m glad that we had two days entry as the 2nd morning the whole area was covered in cloud so you couldn’t see the site but we waited at the top of the mountain and it cleared for a beautiful view. There are some bad reviews of routes limiting access but you can see from my photos that it also means your visit won’t look like a market and you can get some lovely photos and enjoy the experience. Some interesting bits I learnt today sharing if interested: The rocks that make Machu Picchu came from part of the mountain that broke off so the area was a quarry of rocks. You can see some of the bigger rocks that make up the Fertility Temple are actually rocks that go right into the mountain. Machu Picchu sits on a mountain made of different types of quartz which is good energy. Hope you enjoy your visit and take a moment to feel the vibes.

text_clean: Sin duda un lugar que sorprende. Encontrar en Lima un lugar arqueológico tan bien cuidado, que sigue en estudio y descubriendo historia es espectacular y maravilloso. Lo recomiendo totalmente, el itinerario a recorrer es muy completo, los guías explican y atienden a las preguntas de una manera amena, amable y con empeño explican cuidadosamente todos los detalles. Lo que menos me ha gustado es que haya animales encerrados en jaulas aunque estén bien cuidados (que lo están, pero que cumplan sus cuidados veterinarios no significa que sea ético ni justificativo), creo que a estas alturas el debate de tener animales para uso recreativo debería estar más que superado. Creo que para explicar al público los animales que convivían con la población preinca no es necesario tener animales vivos. Creo que ayudaría mucho a que instituciones que pertenecen al estado peruano, den ese ejemplo a la ciudadania y turistas.

text_clean: The visit to Machu Picchu was amazing and breath taking. advisable to wear comfy clothing and sneakers. If your not used to the high altitude u will get short of breath easily so make sure you have severeal hours available to pace your climb. there is only 1 bathroom area in the entrance so make sure you use the facilities before hand if not you will have to go down and climb back up. use sunblock and bring hats to block sun it can get really hot even in the winter. no food is sold there so pack light snacks if neeed for young children and bring water bottles. food is not supposed to be consumed there so be discreet and make sure you disposes of trash properly. i would recommend going there in off season because lines waits were over 1 hour to get on the bus that brings u from aguas calientes to machu picchu and vise versa. and getting really good pictures was hard due to the amount of visitors, was very crowded. we went the 3rd week in July which is during high season. before paying for a guide check reviews because i felt like our guide rushed through and didn't explain as much as some of the other guides i overheard. have fun and enjoy.

text_clean: We spent an afternoon and the better part of the following day at MP. The two days we were there the clouds were rolling over the mountain tops and created an incredible, life experience type of view. I recommend getting a guide to best understand what you are viewing and the history. This place is not easy to get to including the long lines to get the bus ride from the valley to the entrance near the top of the mountain (only way to get there). Crowds are an issue both in MP and getting up there. We stayed at the small lodge right by the entrance which helped greatly in our enjoyment due to missing some of the crowds and avoiding two bus rides. Expensive and hard to get a room but worth it if your budget allows. The views are incredible, breath taking, etc... The place defies imagination as to how the Incas were able to construct it.

text_clean: Vale la pena pasear en el teleférico, cómodo, seguro, sin colas y un excelente servicio... Pero, una vez que bajas de la cabina se acabó la buena experiencia, no te explican que tienes que hacer una caminata de aproximadamente 1 hora hasta las ruinas (kilómetro y medio de subidas y bajadas), por un camino donde los primeros 200 metros están bien conservados y el resto es un desastre. Al llegar a la fortaleza, supuestamente, tienes que haber sacado un ticket en línea y solo deberían entrar 13 personas a la vez por 45 minutos. En mi turno dejaron entrar como a 30. Osea, por las puras uno saca un turno. Los 45 minutos son en verdad 15, por qué lo que está habilitado para recorrer es solo una mínima parte. Bueno, en verdad es a criterio de los de seguridad, por qué habia un trayecto mayor para recorrer pero sin mayor explicación, solo el YO estoy diciendo que no se puede ir por allá, te impiden hacer un mayor recorrido. Si no está listo para recibir turismo, por qué no lo cierran y se dedican a hacer las cosas bien.

text_clean: 500+ year old ruins of Incan imperial retreat. Stunning views, amazing history, good workout. Bring bug spray (mites/gnats will devour your legs). Walking sticks are helpful. Not wheelchair accessible, and extremely difficult for people with mobility challenges. Staff are friendly and helpful. One of the most photogenic places on earth. Visited on 1st Oct. Sunny at 9am, overcast at 10am, raining at 11am, and sunny again by noon. Wasn't too terribly crowded, but everyone moves at their own pace, so sometimes you pass someone, other times you get passed. Llamas at the top of Circuit 2... not aggressive, will let you pet them if you take your time. Food and toilets available at the trailhead. No hired guides necessary. Queues for the bus up and back down are inconceivably long! Bring bottled water!

text_clean: Visited on 4/24/24, which was my birthday, and it was one of the most epic experiences of my life. It was so amazing that we decided to go back the following day as well. There’s little that hasn’t already been said about this location and the history is well known so I will spare that part of my review but honestly, this is a destination that everyone should visit at least once in their life. I saw very young people all the way up to people in their 90s so it really is possible for anyone except those with mobility or health issues. The mystery and beauty of Machu Picchu are reasons enough to visit, but just being there and walking the grounds you really feel connected to history and the views are absolutely breathtaking. Our first day we went with a guide and the second time we went by ourselves. Both were great experiences. You can walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu but I would suggest taking a bus because it will take multiple hours to get up there. You can also take the Inka Trail (3 nights and 4 days) and hike down into Machu Picchu so there are multiple different methods of getting there. We decided to stay in Aguas Calientes for a few nights so we took the bus to and from each visit. Our first day we went with a guide who took us around but the second day we decided to choose Circuit 2 which many have said is the best route. It is definitely the most comprehensive and you get to see most of the site this way. We also added the Inka Bridge which was closed when we visited, but we were able to get very close to it. It’s an extra half mile climb once you reach the top of Machu Picchu but honestly it’s pretty easy, however, I would avoid it if you are afraid of heights because the pathway there is very narrow and the drop off to the side is hundreds, if not over 1000, feet to the bottom. I am still in awe of everything I saw and it really left me with an immense appreciation and respect for the Peruvian people and what the Inca accomplished. I could not recommend this site enough and feel that it definitely deserves the UNESCO world heritage site badge as well as being one of the new Seven Wonders of the World.

text_clean: Arriving here at 6 am and seeing the fog slowly lift off the citadel and the surrounding mountains was the highlight of our entire trip. Even with all the hype, the place was bigger and better than what I expected. We spent the first few hours touring the citadel and then did the Wayna Picchu hike. We were only able to get out once to use the restrooms which was a little stressful, because you do want to stay hydrated while hiking and eventually need to pee and the bathrooms are located outside the exit which is all the way across the citadel from WP entrance. We also could have done with a better guide, we may have not had good luck with ours. He repeated himself several times and did not seem to give us many details. Our ollantaytambo tour guide did a better job and we were able to understand the symbolism from his descriptions to us than our MP guide which was a little disappointing.

text_clean: Unfortunately had 2 days (visiting afternoon then morning on consecutive days) when weather was not good. 1st day in particular very wet (rain) and no "long views" due to heavy mist. Second day was better in terms of less rain/mist but being Sunday(?) it was VERY busy. All that said, this is truly a 'magical' space and is best visited (at least) twice, once with a guide but the 2nd time without thereby allowing the opportunity to spend longer at points of 'personal' interest AND as in my case, being able to explore parts that are pointed out by guides but not visited, due to time constraints. Beware the bus up in the morning will entail long queues (although buses are frequent) and it's similar in the afternoon coming down. Be prepared with all relevant documentation to avoid being stopped from boarding (bus) or entering site. Also be aware that movement around the site tends to be 'one way' and enforced by "guards" with whistles!

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

text_clean: Pero vaya que lugar tan increíble, nunca antes lo había visitado y siempre veía y escuchaba hablar del museo, por lo que siempre estaba en mi lista de lugares del que tenía que visitar y ya lo hice pero a pesar de ello, volvería a visitarlo una vez más; porque tiene muchos detalles e historia por aprender y un día no basta. text_clean: English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

text_clean: There are no words to accurately describe the majesty of this city in the clouds. As you enter into the city, you feel transported to a new world especially in the morning before the fog has faded. We spent two days at the city. On the first, we hiked Waynapicchu. It is a difficult climb especially at fairly high elevation but you can take your time. If you are able, you must do this hike. It was the highlight of our Peru trip. Sadly, some of the sites were closed for renovation including the Moon Temple. On the second, we explored the city with a tour guide. We booked ours through Adios Adventure Travel and they were great though a little pricy. Even when exploring the city, prepare for a lot of walking and stairs. Llamas (and a few alpacas) wander aroud freely. Make sure you use the restroom prior to entering the city. There is no in and out privilege. You probably should take the bus from Aguas Calientes to Macchu Picchu especially if you expect the walk around Macchu Picchu to be strenous and tiring already. We walked down the mountain hoping to see an Andea bear. It was still beautiful but not a crucial hike. Make sure you purchase your tickets for Macchu Picchu in advance. There are a very limited amount of tickets sold at the gate hence the protests. We were able to avoid waiting in long lines and have security knowing our spot was booked by buying tickets several weeks prior.

On the other hand stay the construction itself : very modest and elementary for the time ( Incas like all American tribes ) were underdeveloped and it is evident in all their monuments around the continent. Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

Hay vigilancia pero de todas maneras hay que tener cuidado muy visitado por turistas en un solo lugar encuentras gran variedad de la gastronomía peruana sobre todo limeña. No recomiendo ir fin de semana hay muchísima gente.

text_clean: Horrible. Hay dos piletas en donde pueden entrar los niños (9 y 13). En una es hasta los 10 años y en otra desde los 13. Colas super largas y vine con mis dos hermanos. En la otra espere 30 minutos e igual no me dejaron entrar porque mi otro hermano ya tenía más de 10 años. Cómo hago? Me divido entonces. Su lógica está mal. Pagando estacionamiento, entradas y comida todo para que mis hermanos terminen llorando porque no entraron juntos. Pésimo.

text_clean: Todo peruano debe conocer este lugar!!! Es excelente y sobre todo barato... Si vienes desde Chachapoyas puedes tomar tours q cobran entre 90 a 110 soles x persona todo incluido, hasta almuerzos... Por cuenta propia tomas una combi a las 6am a 15 soles aprox hasta el distrito de Nuevo Tingo, luego caminas 15 minutos hasta el local de embarque de transporte para el ingreso hacia los TELEFÉRICOS donde compras tú entrada (20.40 soles precio, incluye ida y vuelta) IMPORTANTE.. NO PERDER LOS TICKETS xq si los pierdes, compras otros para el regreso al precio inicial... una vez pagado, esperas tú turno y ellos te llevan en bus hasta la zona elevada del cerro para subir por fin a los telefericos, el trayecto es de 20 minutos aprox, luego te hacerlas a la casa amarilla donde pagas tú entrada a 30 soles y te permiten subir el camino de 2 km q demoran unos 45 minutos... Ya en la cima vaz a la derecha hasta el ingreso a la fortaleza donde entregas la entrada a compraste a 30 soles... Una vez adentro, DISFRUTA DEL PASEO, debes calcular tus tiempos xq el ticket del teleférico te menciona la hora de regreso... Así q atento... Igual te dan tolerancia pero si hay mucha gente puede q se compliquen los tiempos pues la cola se hace grande al regreso. Pásenla chvrs causitas! Sí desean alguien que les guíe en el tour les dejo el número de contacto del guía que sale en la foto de chaleco beige

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: Increíble lugar, aunque sea una vez en la vida debemos visitar! Para visitar MP, sacar las entradas con varios días de anticipación, ahora se hace todo desde la web de Machu Picchu, se puede pagar con tarjeta o en las oficinas que están cerca de la plaza Regocijo. Atención con el horario, porque solo se puede entrar en el horario especifico. Aun así, si te toca el último turno y no te conviene, puedes entrar hasta las 7:25 am siempre y cuando vayas con un guía. El guía cobra 20 soles por persona o 100 por un grupo familiar. Ya estando allí, habrá varios guardianes que verán que no se saquen el tapabocas en ningún momento. Lo niños tienen más tolerancia en ese aspecto. Está todo muy bien cuidado y señalizado. El detalle que malogra todo el paseo es el tren local, que como turistas nacionales tenemos la opción de tomar . Sin embargo, la tensión por conseguir un boleto es terrible, no debería ser así ya que al final todos viajan, la caseta de serenazgo te da un turno y con eso deberías evitar colas. Hay mucha especulación y también eso da pie para que algunos taxistas te quieran llevar a otras estaciones tratando de sacar ventaja e incluso engañar a los turistas. Tengan cuidado con eso. Fuera de eso, todo excelente 👍

Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: Sin duda puedes disfrutarlo más de noche. Eh visto algunos extranjeros, pero también eh visto a niños perdidos, a gente en pésimo estado, hay mucho perdidos, hace MUCHA FALTA la seguridad. Esa noche de las fotos que fui, un domingo por la noche con mi familia , vi a un hombre atacando a dos mujeres con gas pimienta. Una de ella lloraba y pedía ayuda a gritos y la otra la ayudaba mientras que grababa al atacante. A unos metros de mi en pleno show histórico de las aguas de las 7pm. Este atacó a la señora eh intentó huir pero solo había uno que otro intentándolo detener y grabándolo. text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: Visitar el Monasterio de Santa Catalina fue, sin exagerar, una de las experiencias más fascinantes que he vivido en Perú. No solo por su belleza arquitectónica, sino por la atmósfera que se respira en cada rincón: una mezcla envolvente de historia, silencio, espiritualidad y arte. Desde el primer momento que crucé sus enormes puertas de piedra, sentí que estaba entrando a otra época. El monasterio es una ciudadela en sí misma, con sus propias calles, pequeñas plazas, fuentes, cocinas, lavanderías y celdas que, en su momento, fueron ocupadas por monjas de clausura. Caminando por los pasillos de piedra volcánica, se puede sentir el aislamiento deliberado del mundo exterior y la devoción con la que vivían allí. Los colores de las paredes son vibrantes: el rojo terracota, el azul profundo y el blanco encalado contrastan perfectamente con la luz intensa del cielo arequipeño. Cada rincón es fotogénico, pero más allá de eso, todo parece cuidadosamente pensado para fomentar la contemplación. A pesar de que ahora es un lugar abierto al público, el silencio sigue predominando, como si el monasterio aún guardara los secretos de las mujeres que vivieron y murieron ahí. Una de las cosas que más me impresionó fue la celda de Sor Ana de los Ángeles Monteagudo, una beata arequipeña cuya habitación permanece casi intacta. También me llamó la atención la sencillez de los objetos que usaban, contrastando con el lujo con el que muchas de estas monjas, provenientes de familias aristocráticas, llegaron al monasterio. La visita guiada (altamente recomendable) me permitió entender mejor la historia del lugar, que data del siglo XVI. El guía explicó con claridad cómo se fue expandiendo el monasterio, cómo cambiaron las reglas de clausura con los años, y cómo ahora una pequeña comunidad de monjas aún vive en una zona restringida del recinto, separada del área turística. En definitiva, el Monasterio de Santa Catalina no es solo un monumento histórico, es una cápsula del tiempo. Me fui con la sensación de haber conocido un pedazo profundo de la historia colonial del Perú, pero también con una extraña paz interior. Es un lugar que invita a caminar despacio, a observar, y a reflexionar. Una joya imperdible en el corazón de Arequipa.

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
