text_clean: One of the best preserved historical sites that I've ever visited, especially considering its ruins lies in Miraflores (a neighborhood in the very heart of Lima). It's really quite an interesting place to visit (went during the day - there are also night tours, please check season and opening times on website) and tickets offer free guided tour every 30 minutes (languages: English, Portuguese, Spanish, Italian, French, and Japanese). Tours often take around 45 - 75 min (unfortunately only way allowed in) as the site has got a lot to see. The ruins complex are divided in 2 sections (A - main pyramid and surrounding buildings, and B - analysis labs and storage space), covering approximately 150 thousands m². There is still an area, aside of the central pyramid, that is being used for planting crops and raising animals, which is believed to have been vital to those people living nearby back in time. To get there, you can either walk, take a bus or a taxi (Ubers are well worth it). Walking distance from Parque del Amor should not be more than 20 minutes. Be aware that it almost never rains in Lima, so you'd better be prepared for the sun and the heat (ruins are open air, stay well hydrated and use loads of sunscreen). Lastly, should let you know that there's a small museum (included on the ticket price), a shop, and a good restaurant onsite (perfect for those who are visiting Lima for one day - it's very expensive though).

Lastly, should let you know that there's a small museum (included on the ticket price), a shop, and a good restaurant onsite (perfect for those who are visiting Lima for one day - it's very expensive though).

text_clean: Machu Picchu is an ancient Incan citadel located high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. The site is one of the most iconic and breathtakingly beautiful destinations in the world, attracting millions of visitors every year. To reach Machu Picchu, visitors can either hike the famous Inca Trail or take a train from the nearby town of Cusco. Once there, visitors can explore the ruins of the citadel, which include impressive structures such as temples, terraces, and plazas. The intricate stonework and engineering feats of the Incas are truly awe-inspiring. While Machu Picchu can get crowded, especially during peak season, there are ways to avoid the crowds and make the most of the experience. One option is to visit early in the morning or later in the afternoon when there are fewer visitors. Another option is to take a guided tour, which can provide insight into the history and significance of the site. Overall, Machu Picchu is a must-see destination for anyone visiting Peru or South America. The beauty, history, and cultural significance of the site are truly unmatched, and it's an experience that will stay with you for a lifetime. text_clean: 6-7 hour bus ride from Arequipa (terminal terrestre, Arturo Ibanez 130) to Cabanaconde. Route over highlands, vicuna reserve, down to Chivay in canyon, then down canyon, (best views from right side of bus). 9:30 AM Arequipa departure (Andelusia) and nights in Cabanaconde before and after self guided trek, seemed better than 3:00 AM Arequipa departure, short last trek day and back to Arequipa of tours. Many places to stay, I was at Pachamama hostel about 2 blocks up from the plaza on San Pedro St. Good information about trek options and good place to meet others doing unguided treks. I did 2d/1n trek, 1st day to Llahuar with scenic descent along canyon edge, then down into side canyon, final descent to Colca river crossing, climb into Taruna, back down to Llahuar on the Colca river just after crossing the Huaruro river. Nice bungalows on river, friendly staff, fixed menu dinner, breakfast and sandwichs available, all at very reasonable prices. Natural hot pools and swimming pool right on river not open because river very high in early March. Very ambitious new "restaurant" construction ongoing. As hike to Llahuar mostly down and not too long, I was there well before noon. Could just chill in nice location. I opted for day hike up Huaruro canyon that starts steeply right from Llahuar then more gradually up to Llatica where a bridge crosses the river. From there another steep climb to high trail that contours back down other side of canyon to above Belen. Nice safe and scenic canyon trails. Down main street in Belen past soccer field to steep trail to Paclla. Cross road and continue on trail down through terraced gardens that eventually goes to Taruna above Llahuar. This added another 11 km and 700m vertical, about 4 hours at a fast pace, to first day. 2nd day back up to Belen, options are trail short cuts to Paclla not too steep, then very steep to Belen, or longer but more gradually walk on road. From Belen you are on road up to viewpoint bend then level to shortcut trail down to plateau, join main trail down to Sangalle (relatively touristy and expensive, but amazing given that everything has to come in by horse or burro). That just leaves the 2.5 - 3.5 hour all uphill switchbacks to plateau and final km to Cabanaconde. Total for 2nd day a healthy 16-18km with 1700m elevation gain. If I was doing it again, would spend 2 nights in canyon, 1st at Llahuar, then 2nd up at Fure with day hike up to the waterfall (cataratas), day 3 high trail back from Fure contouring back to join road at high point above Sangalle, then through Sangalle up and out. Another interesting high altitude strenuous option might be continuing up from Fure to Tocallo at 3800m where some maps show a trail that goes in and out of several canyons with final pass then long descent to Malata above Sangalle. Another night in canyon and some local information about condition of trails would be needed. Canyon country was unique, scenic and very green with lots of water in the rainy season. Lodging and meals in many areas down in canyon allows for very light packs, a plus given all the climbs. No reservations needed for anything during rainy season, probably a different story in high season. A minibus service that goes on the not too scary dirt road from Carbanaconde, crossing the Rio Colca at the same place my trek did has beautiful brand new rain sheltered stops at all the little towns all the way to Tapay! This allows some less strenuous options for the canyon that are sure to become more popular.

text_clean: Machu Picchu ⛰ La forma más barata de visitar Machu Picchu es teniendo tiempo: ⛰ De Cusco tomar un transfer hasta Ollantaytambo temprano en la mañana y el trayecto dura 1 hora con 30 minutos. La agencia te recoge en el hospedaje. ⛰ Luego otro viaje en tren local y como es tan solicitado por los propios Peruanos, toca hacer la reserva con tiempo para poder encontrar cupos; la prioridad es para los locales. ⛰ Para subir a las ruinas, el paquete incluye la entrada pero si llegas por ti mismo; existen dos formas: la paga y la gratis. Puedes usar el bus que vale para extranjeros 12 dólares por trayecto. Aceptan efectivo y tarjeta. El viaje tarda 25 minutos. ⛰ La forma gratis es subir toda la montaña en un sendero señalizado y la subida dura casi tres horas. La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.

text_clean: Es un convento que te permiten revisar sin limitación la mayoría de los recintos donde vivían las religiosas de la ciudad... rico en historia además que aprendemos de la vida de Sor Ana de Los Angeles y disfrutamos de su exquisita repostería (hay una cafetería con venta de postres y bebidas, es obvio, precio un poco elevado que afuera del monasterio). No tiene estacionamiento. No apto para silla de ruedas. Hay servicios higiénicos pequeños. El ingreso es 45 soles (un poco caro según mi opinión)... en el caso de peruanos hay promoción el último domingo de cada mes es de 15 soles mostrando tu DNI. Se puede adquirir el boleto de forma online o presencial. Al ingreso, te ofrecen el servicio de guía por todo el monasterio. Eres libre de elegir o rechazar. Ahi mismo hay un código QR para descargar una app que te muestra información del monasterio y un mapa del local. Además que en cada habitación o calle existe una descripción en diferentes idiomas. Tiempo aproximado de estancia mínimo 1 hora si vas sólo a tomarte fotos... voy ingresando varias veces con amigos extranjeros que les gusta la historia y añadido las fotografias sin personas de fondo en las calles, es de 2 horas aproximadamente. Recomendado 😊

text_clean: En mi estadía en Arequipa, contraté un tour de un día al cañón del Colca. Madrugamos a las tres de la mañana para viajar a nuestro destino. Son aproximadamente tres horas y media desde el centro de Arequipa hasta el cañón. Es importante advertir la altura a la que se llega. En el mirador del Vuelo del Cóndor hay 3 lugares para observar el paisaje y el vuelo propiamente dicho, y para ir de mirador en mirador hay tramos largos por donde subir y bajar. Por lo que es recomendable no agitarse mucho ni apresurarse para evitar fatigas. Estás fotos reflejan la vista de los viajes entre destino y destino en todo el tour. Sin duda uno de los más bellos regalos dados por nuestra madre naturaleza.

text_clean: Totally worth the hype. Amazing to see in person. Tips we wished we’d known: Get there at 6 for the misty views that then open up into clear sights. Stay for a few hours at one of the viewpoints because it’s a one-way, no re-entry system. Go for circuit 2 for the best views. Get to the bus stop early as the queues start an hour before. The weather changes super fast so pack rain gear. You can book your Machu Picchu ticket MUCH cheaper on the website rather than through a tour. Either bring plenty of money for food as it’s very expensive or bring pack-ups. Go to the cascades for a swim while in aguas calientes. text_clean: Visited on 4/24/24, which was my birthday, and it was one of the most epic experiences of my life. It was so amazing that we decided to go back the following day as well. There’s little that hasn’t already been said about this location and the history is well known so I will spare that part of my review but honestly, this is a destination that everyone should visit at least once in their life. I saw very young people all the way up to people in their 90s so it really is possible for anyone except those with mobility or health issues. The mystery and beauty of Machu Picchu are reasons enough to visit, but just being there and walking the grounds you really feel connected to history and the views are absolutely breathtaking. Our first day we went with a guide and the second time we went by ourselves. Both were great experiences. You can walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu but I would suggest taking a bus because it will take multiple hours to get up there. You can also take the Inka Trail (3 nights and 4 days) and hike down into Machu Picchu so there are multiple different methods of getting there. We decided to stay in Aguas Calientes for a few nights so we took the bus to and from each visit. Our first day we went with a guide who took us around but the second day we decided to choose Circuit 2 which many have said is the best route. It is definitely the most comprehensive and you get to see most of the site this way. We also added the Inka Bridge which was closed when we visited, but we were able to get very close to it. It’s an extra half mile climb once you reach the top of Machu Picchu but honestly it’s pretty easy, however, I would avoid it if you are afraid of heights because the pathway there is very narrow and the drop off to the side is hundreds, if not over 1000, feet to the bottom. I am still in awe of everything I saw and it really left me with an immense appreciation and respect for the Peruvian people and what the Inca accomplished. I could not recommend this site enough and feel that it definitely deserves the UNESCO world heritage site badge as well as being one of the new Seven Wonders of the World.

text_clean: Arriving here at 6 am and seeing the fog slowly lift off the citadel and the surrounding mountains was the highlight of our entire trip. We also could have done with a better guide, we may have not had good luck with ours. He repeated himself several times and did not seem to give us many details. Our ollantaytambo tour guide did a better job and we were able to understand the symbolism from his descriptions to us than our MP guide which was a little disappointing.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

text_clean: Stunning site, but plan ahead Machu Picchu is breathtaking and absolutely worth the trip. However, the logistics can be tricky. We waited a frustrating 18 hours for tickets because we didn’t book in advance—big mistake! Entrance fees are fair, but additional costs like the ~$24 round-trip bus and extra charges for guides quickly add up. And of course you need to calculate a round-trip train+bus tickets from Cusco, too. Despite the crowds and commercialization, this place is pure magic and it’s worth every step. If possible, go later in the day when the crowds thin and the light hits just right. Machu Picchu will stay with you long after you’ve left—but only if you avoid the ticket fiasco, so go prepared and you’ll have the time of your life! TIPS: - Book tickets months ahead and hire a guide at the entrance - Take the bus from Aguas Calientes unless you’re up for a steep 2-hour hike. - Circuit 2 offers the best views and flexibility. - Bring water, sunscreen, and a camera, you’ll need all three.

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.

text_clean: Machu Picchu - 10 things that may help to make your trip more pleasurable updated: 25 September 2022 1. Book entry tickets well in advance - there is a daily quota of 2,000 people only 2. Decide on pace of travel - day trip from Cusco is doable but it’s still at least a 12-hour ordeal door to door. To have a less stressful visit if time allows, stay in fun Aguas Calientes to chill for an evening or 2 before embarking on your choice of transportation is a pretty good idea. 3. Decide on how you want to get there - a). Hiking all the way (28 miles for 4d3n) b). Train all the way (Inkarail / Perurail for 1.5 hrs) c). Train and Hike combo (train then hike the last 8 miles) d). Bus 4. Decide on when you want to get there - a). For Sunrise, be prepared to stay a night in Machu Picchu (national park opens at 6am) b). For Sunset, time yourself to find a nice seat facing west near the sundial. Remember you have 3 hours inside the park and you will need to get yourself out after sunset. 5. Local tour guide is essential - the last chance to find them if you do not already have one will be in the bus queue from Machu Picchu train station or outside the national park. Apart from facts and myths you can read up prior to the visit, the guide will be able to point out interesting facts such as: best spots to take photographs / the stones that look like a llama. 6. Separate bookings / time restricted visits - a). Visit to the sundial is included in the tickets for national park entry but it’s only opened from 7-10am daily b). Wayna Picchu (Young Mountain) , Hucchu Picchu (Baby Mountain) & Gran Caverna: this requires booking separately. There are 2 groups of 200 pax daily capacity. Round trip time takes around 2 hrs / 45 min / 4 hrs respectively. 7. Mosquito repellent: this is absolutely essential if you do not want to remember the breath taking view of Machu Picchu by the itchy mozzie scars! These bugs are tiny black things, and they draw blood in nano seconds! Army grade DEET is recommended especially if you are a sunrise / sunset romantic, or if you intend to do any form of hiking. The bites may only appear up to 36 hours later! 8. Climate check: a). check whether but be prepared to encounter all 4 seasons on your visit b). check political actions - as protests can shut down all roads and transportations! 9. Be prepared to queue: no pain no gain! 10. Altitude: if you are like me whose normal inhabitant is at sea level, climatise yourself gently. Cusco is at 3400m and although Michu Picchu is at a lower altitude of 2400m, strolling can still be taxing at times! Hope you have a wonderful trip like I did!

text_clean: First advise: arrive the day before you want to visit, stay in the small village, get up really early and take the first bus to this site to avoid the large crowds. I was there early and could enjoy the remains of what once must have been a beautiful well organised Inca citadel. It lies on a ridge 2430m above sealevel. As the Inca's did not keep written language records the purpose of the site has long been unknown but is now believed to have been the estate of Inca emperor Pachacuti It must have been a tremendous engineering and construction task to build this and a challenge to provide food and fresh water to it's inhabitants And it reminds us that no civilisation is forever.

text_clean: My Peru experience was amazing and undertaking the Inca Trail to Machu Picchu one of the most wonderful experiences of my life. Camping and waking up to such beautiful mountain views in the Andes, will stay with me forever. It's a tough walk but one I'd recommend to anyone wanting to see and feel some of our beautiful planet. To finally arrive at the Sun Gate and see Machu Picchu for the first time, at dawn, just, wow. The amusing part is then walking around this breathtaking place, after 5 days of basically living rough, filthy and covered in bug bites amongst all the clean tourists who have got the train up, makes you feel quite self conscious lol!! So I'd say walking the Inca Trail is the ultimate way to experience this, if you can. If not, a day trip via train is certainly to be recommended just to get to see Machu Picchu itself.

text_clean: You should be well prepared but also come with extra time and money if you really want to see Machu Picchu. If you buy tickets months in advance think carefully about the day, time and route since this can not be changed. No matter what you read basically 90% of the guid3d tour offers do NOT have the ticket included, you need to be 100% sure that your offer does or your visit will be a nightmare. Also: if booked far on advance make sure you do not visit in rainy season or your pictures will not show much of Machu Picchu. If you plan to be more flexible and buy the tickets when you are in Aguas Calientes... and the tickets can only be bought there, no matter what you read elsewhere, be prepared for a lot of queueing. We queued on first day to get a number for the queue the second day in the morning. There we git a number for the same day in the evening and there we could then actually buy the ticket for the next day, so the third day we were there. Since we booked the train only one way and waited to book return until we knew our day and time to see Machu Picchu we could not return on day three and had to book a third night and left o day four. text_clean: Pristine from start to finish. The train is a clean and comfortable 3.5-4 hour ride from Cusco. Following the train ride, you will enter Machu Pichu City where you will take a short bus ride to the historical site. Please beware, you will still have a fairly strenuous walk/hike from the bus to the site. If you live in a flat area, I suggest using a stair climber to prepare yourself. My tour, which I booked through AirBnB experiences was a one on one, personalized experience. This allowed me to rest when needed, ask questions, and make correlations. Most of the groups were 20+ people, I don't suggest that. You want to observe and absorb, so book an intimate tour. Also, important to note, there are 3 tour levels. Level one only grants access to having your picture taken close to the site, without touring the site. Level 2 grants the most access overall, but it does not focus on the royalty sites like Level 3. There is also an option to add a hike to Winchu Pichu (the mountain in the background of the pictures). That option is only available twice per day. I would never, so I'm not sure how to book it 😆🤣😆🤣 PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE bring only minimal gear, otherwise you will be rucking. A sweat suit with poncho or a lightweight all weather jacket will suffice. Layer the clothing so you can add/subtract as needed. Apply bug spray before leaving your hotel. Carry only one bottle of water. Fanny pack + water is all you need. You can buy snacks before/after in the city. YOU MUST HAVE YOUR PASSPORT TO ENTER/ EXIT THE SITE. P.S. entry is limited to a few thousand people per day, please book well in advance to secure your spot. Also, the rainy season starts in October. I was very blessed to only endure a sprinkle. With a hard rain, you won't be able to see and it will be very dangerous. You are walking/hiking on stones the entire time.

text_clean: Absolutely Breathtaking – A Once-in-a-Lifetime Experience Visiting Machu Picchu is truly unforgettable. The moment you see the citadel appear among the misty peaks, you understand why it’s considered one of the world’s greatest wonders. The setting is surreal: ancient stone terraces and temples perched high in the Andes, surrounded by lush green mountains and drifting clouds. The site itself is remarkably well-preserved and gives a powerful sense of the Incan civilization’s engineering genius. The stonework, agricultural terraces, and spiritual sites like the Sun Temple and Intihuatana Stone are awe-inspiring. Walking through the ruins feels like stepping back in time. Practical tips: arriving early in the morning is magical, as the sunrise over the mountains adds to the mystique (and it’s less crowded). Be prepared for lots of walking and steep steps, so good shoes are a must. The altitude can be tiring, so pace yourself and stay hydrated. Overall, Machu Picchu is not just a destination—it’s an experience that combines history, culture, and breathtaking natural beauty. An absolute must-see if you’re in Peru.

text_clean: Machu Pichhu Visit – A Truly Unforgettable Experience! Our trip to Machu Pichhu, organised by our friend Shoba Kalyan from Canada, was perfectly planned and combined with tours in Argentina and Brazil. We stayed in comfortable 4-star hotels and had excellent guides throughout. We arrived in Cusco after a delayed flight and took Diamox to handle the high altitude (Cusco is 11,200 ft above sea level). The city tour was fascinating, with beautiful Inca ruins and colonial charm. From Ollantaytambo, we took a scenic 1 hour 40-minute train ride to Machu Pichhu town. The station was crowded and the platform low, but our hotel helped with luggage transport, which was a relief. Machu Pichhu itself is incredible—an ancient 15th-century Inca citadel with amazing stone terraces, temples aligned with the stars, and stunning mountain views. Our guide was knowledgeable, explaining the site’s history, engineering, and spiritual significance. The weather turned rainy and misty during our trek, which made walking tricky, so I highly recommend bringing a good raincoat even in the dry season. Despite the rain, the experience was magical and well worth it. Highly recommend visiting Machu Pichhu with a good guide and proper preparation

text_clean: Fue la razón de mi viaje a Perú. Subí dos veces en diferentes dias. Un dia compartiendo el guía y la siguiente vez con guía privado. Gran diferencia. El lugar por si mismo vale el viaje, las incomodidad de los horarios para tomar el avión a Cusco. Ya que no hay vuelos directos de México hacia allá. Por fuerza tienes que hacer escala en Lima. Vale la pena después de haber pagado un boleto de tren tan caro de Oyataitambo hacia Aguascalientes. Haber pagado una noche de habitación en ese lugar. Todo eso valió la pena. La subida en el bus hacia el lugar del Machupicchu simplemente fascinante. Las vistas desde dentro de los vestigios son maravillosas. El pensar como construyeron toda esa ciudadela, es algo que te deja asombrado. En fin, creo que es uno de los lugares que me he quedado mas que asombrado.. No se lo pierdan...

text_clean: Machu Picchu, término quechua cuyo significado es Montaña vieja. Hoy es universalmente conocida, tanto por sus imponentes restos arqueológicos, como por su incomparable ubicación, al borde de un abismo en cuyo fondo corren las caudalosas aguas del Río Urubamba. Los boletos tienen precios diferenciados, para peruanos, países miembros de la comunidad andina, y otros extranjeros. Pueden llegar caminando desde Machupichu pueblo (creo que dura una hora el camino), o en bus que cobra $12 dólares por trayecto me parece demasiado caro, pero es eso o caminar, los boletos los compran cerca de la estación de tren. Hay 4 circuitos para escoger, nosotros escogimos el circuito 1 y vimos gran parte de la ciudad. No es obligatorio ir con guía. Nosotros no íbamos a contratar guía, pero fue necesario porque fui con un pie fracturado y la guía fue de gran ayuda para decirnos por donde ir, nos explicó todo y nos hizo buenas fotos. Fue una excelente decisión haber contratado a Graciela (está en las fotos, se las recomiendo totalmente). Nos hizo un tour privado por $50 dólares. Hay algunas zonas que se pueden visitar antes de las 10am (reposo del sol) y otras que se pueden visitar después de las 10am. No se puede comer dentro del recinto. Tampoco se puede usar paraguas, lleven su chubasquero porque posiblemente lo necesiten. Afuera hay un restaurante, no se que vendan ni los precios porque no lo visitamos. En el recinto se respira una paz inexplicable, disfruten su estadía.

Hoy es universalmente conocida, tanto por sus imponentes restos arqueológicos, como por su incomparable ubicación, al borde de un abismo en cuyo fondo corren las caudalosas aguas del Río Urubamba. Los boletos tienen precios diferenciados, para peruanos, países miembros de la comunidad andina, y otros extranjeros. Pueden llegar caminando desde Machupichu pueblo (creo que dura una hora el camino), o en bus que cobra $12 dólares por trayecto me parece demasiado caro, pero es eso o caminar, los boletos los compran cerca de la estación de tren. Hay 4 circuitos para escoger, nosotros escogimos el circuito 1 y vimos gran parte de la ciudad. No es obligatorio ir con guía. Nosotros no íbamos a contratar guía, pero fue necesario porque fui con un pie fracturado y la guía fue de gran ayuda para decirnos por donde ir, nos explicó todo y nos hizo buenas fotos. Fue una excelente decisión haber contratado a Graciela (está en las fotos, se las recomiendo totalmente). Nos hizo un tour privado por $50 dólares. Hay algunas zonas que se pueden visitar antes de las 10am (reposo del sol) y otras que se pueden visitar después de las 10am. No se puede comer dentro del recinto. Tampoco se puede usar paraguas, lleven su chubasquero porque posiblemente lo necesiten. Afuera hay un restaurante, no se que vendan ni los precios porque no lo visitamos. En el recinto se respira una paz inexplicable, disfruten su estadía.

Da miedo pensar que puedan incluso invadir espacios ancestrales, históricos por contar con el dinero para obtener servicios "premium" y construir tanto lujo tan cerca. Es un lugar que hay que aprrciar, respetar y cuidar, ojalá no se olvide.

text_clean: Experiencia muy agradable Tomamos el tren desde Cusco, el cual tiene a mi gusto un precio alto, buenas vistas pero las 5 horas de duración se hacen un poco tediosas, los asientos son cómodos pero no incluye nada más, Una vez llegamos a aguas Calientes, el lugar es encantador, Existen cientos de restaurantes, pero les recomiendo los que están a la Orilla del Río, algo más caros, pero con un sabor q vale la pena, Los restaurantes internos de las calles tienen precios de 25 soles por persona, pero el sabor no es bueno, y eso que probamos varios, además la porción es pequeña, y a veces el refresco cuesta lo mismo q el plato y no va incluido. Es mejor pagar en los otros 40-60 soles por plato con un gran sabor En cuanto a hospedaje ahí en Aguas Calientes, donde te deja el tren para el día siguiente ir a Machu Picchu, hay bastantes, no veo razón para gastar demasiado, todos cerca y algunos de arriba en las calles son bonitos y acogedores Al día siguiente tomamos el bus hacia Machu Picchu, una barbaridad 24 dólares ida y regreso Pero sino quieres caminar es la única forma, aunque creo están quitando la concesión a la operadora de los buses. Debes comprarlo con tiempo La entrada a Machu Picchu debe ser comprada con tiempo, pq se agotan los horarios más cotizados y son varias rutas, 1. Asegúrate de q entres temprano, pq hay una sección que se cierra a las 10am y los q llegan luego no pueden pasar, 2. Asegúrate q tu recorrido es el q necesitas hacer, hay varios, y el q me gusta es el completo 3. No incluye la entrada a las montañas, Waynapicchu, y otras rutas, esa aventura cuesta 50 dólares más, En la fila muchos guías ofrecen sus servicios, la verdad vale la pena ya arriba, pero comienzan con precios muy altos de 60 soles por persona, debes tener en cuenta que conforme se les agotan las oportunidades bajan hasta 20-30 soles x persona, si saben bien de la ruta y de lo que hablan, vale la pena, En general todo tarda unas 3-4 horas Muy recomendado, en esta época Julio, mucho sol, muy caliente.
